Title: Linear Programming Approach to Deceptive Path Planning Game with Goal Selection

URL Source: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2605.16548v1

Published Time: Tue, 19 May 2026 00:16:18 GMT

Number of Pages: 7

Markdown Content:
## Linear Programming Approach to Deceptive Path Planning Game with Goal Selection 

Violetta Rostobaya 1, Yue Guan 2, James Berneburg 1 and Daigo Shishika 1

Abstract — In adversarial settings, a mobile agent may strate-gically plan its motion to influence an opponent’s inference about its intended goal. We study deceptive path planning in a scenario where a mobile agent aims to reach a pri-vately selected goal while an adversarial observer allocates limited defensive resources based on the observed trajectory. Unlike classical path-planning and goal-recognition approaches that model observers as passive inference process, our game-theoretic formulation models them as strategic decision-makers. For the resulting dynamic asymmetric-information game, we develop an efficient solution method that combines a linear programming formulation with the Double Oracle algorithm. To evaluate performance, we introduce metrics that quantify both the risk and the effectiveness of deception and provide illustrative numerical examples. 

I. I NTRODUCTION 

Deception plays an important role in domains such as mili-tary operations [1], security planning [2], and autonomous systems operating in contested environments [3]. In this paper, we study deception in the context of motion planning, where a mobile agent seeks to reach a privately selected tar-get while a surveilling opponent allocates limited defensive resources to candidate goals as illustrated in Fig. 1. This setting is closely related to goal recognition [4]–[6], in which an observer infers a mobile agent’s intent from its trajectory, typically assuming that the agent follows a cost-minimizing plan. In deceptive path planning (DPP), goal-recognition models are often used to specify how an observer infers an agent’s intent, against which the mobile agent optimizes its trajectory to reach its true goal while reducing inference accuracy [7]–[10]. However, these works often treat the steering of observer’s beliefs as the primary objective without considering its effect on the observer’s decision making. We address this gap by formulating these deceiver-observer interactions as games with explicit models of observer’s action. Prior works on game-theoretic formulation of DPP con-sidered different environments, information structures, and/or equilibrium concepts. The initial work that introduced a game with a resource-allocating observer considered a grid-world environment with worst-case payoff [11]. This work was later extended to general graph environments with zero-sum Bayesian formulation [12], where the goal was randomly

The Attacker’s perspective <latexit sha1_base64="9+hHWXdFdHQWbN0Ek5IpOTWSRUU="></latexit> 

The Defender’s perspective 

Fig. 1. The Attacker aims to reach its selected goal g1. The Defender does not know the true goal, but it aims to infer the Attacker’s intentions and allocate maximum amount of defensive resources to the true goal. 

assigned to the agent with a known prior probability. Both works assumed that the deceiver must reach one of two candidate goals (i.e., there are two possible types of agents). In contrast, we generalize the DPP game to a multi-goal scenario. Moreover, we allow the deceiver to select its goal at the start of the game, thereby eliminating the need to assume a prior probability distribution over goals. Computational complexity is another important consider-ation for the DPP game. Prior works have leveraged specific geometric structure to induce tractable solutions [11], [12]. More generally, dynamic imperfect-information games such as ours that allow game-tree representation may be solved us-ing a linear programming (LP) [13] or bilinear program [14] under certain structural assumptions. Specifically, zero-sum asymmetric-information games can be solved with LP when the state is controlled solely by the informed player [15]. However, representing the game in extensive form leads to a game tree whose size grows exponentially with the planning horizon, making direct solution methods computationally challenging for large environments. To address this computational challenge, we adapt the Double Oracle algorithm developed for finite extensive-form games (XDO) [16], which incrementally expands the game tree until it is sufficient for an ε-Nash equilibrium. In particular, we combine this approach with the LP formu-lation [15], yielding an iterative method that is well-suited for our multi-goal DPP game. Leveraging the one-sided incomplete-information structure and a particular choice of 

default strategies [16], [17], we are able to solve DPP game with indefinite horizon (i.e., the termination time is not specified apriori) and compute an exact Nash equilibrium rather than an ε-Nash equilibrium. The main contributions of this work are: (i) formulation of a new variant of the DPP game with goal selection, 

> arXiv:2605.16548v1 [eess.SY] 15 May 2026

(ii) solution of the game via an algorithm that integrates the LP formulation with the Double Oracle algorithm for extensive-form games; and (iii) metrics to evaluate risk and effectiveness of emergent deceptive behavior, benchmarked against the complete-information setting. II. P RELIMINARIES 

We consider an asymmetric information deceptive path plan-ning game between a mobile agent, Attacker, and an observ-ing agent, Defender, as illustrated in Fig. 1. The Attacker moves in a graph environment containing multiple candidate goals. At the start of the game, the Attacker selects a goal that it must reach. The Defender, lacking knowledge of the selected goal, must infer it based solely on observations of the Attacker’s trajectory. The Defender seeks to allocate defense resources to the selected goal before the Attacker arrives. This setting naturally incentivizes the Attacker to move in a way that conceals its true goal and misleads the Defender into allocating resources to the wrong goals. 

Environment: The graph is denoted as G = ⟨V , E, w ⟩,where V is a finite set of nodes, and E ⊆ V × V is the set of edges. We use N (s) to represent the out-neighbors of 

s on G. The positive edge weight is given by the function 

w : E → R>0. The weight w(s, s ′) represents the amount of defense resource the Defender can allocate to one of the goals while the Attacker is traversing through edge (s, s ′).The set of candidate goals is denoted by Θ ⊆ V . At the beginning of the game, the Attacker selects a goal θ ∈ Θ.An Attacker that selects goal θ is said to be of type θ. Once the goal is selected, the Attacker must commit to it for the remainder of the game; that is, the Attacker’s type remains fixed. 

Dynamics: The game state at time t is given by the Attacker’s position st ∈ V . At t = −1, the Attacker makes a pre-game decision and selects its type from the set Θ. This choice is not revealed to the Defender and remains private to the Attacker till the end of the game. At t ≥ 0, the Attacker with type θ chooses its next position st+1 from its action set:

The DPP game presented above is a zero-sum stochastic game with asymmetric information in which the state is controlled only by the informed agent, the Attacker. This setting allows the use of the LP method presented in [15]. However, a key difference is that while [15] considers games with known and fixed terminal time, our DPP game has free terminal time: i.e., the game termination depends on the Attacker’s strategy. Therefore, in Section III-A, we firstly provide an LP method to solve a restricted game , which is a prefix-closed truncation of the original extensive-form game to a finite set of histories. In Section III-B, we secondly provide a Double-Oracle algorithm inspired by [16], [17] that iteratively expands the restricted game to find the solution for the overall unrestricted game. 

A. Solution for a restricted game. 

We first analyze a restricted game, which is a prefix-closed truncation of the original extensive-form game to a set of histories ˆH ⊂ S∞ 

> t=0

Ht. By definition of prefix-closedness, if a history ht+1 belongs to ˆH, then its prefix ht also belongs to ˆH. We obtain the initial restricted game by propagating the game tree up to a fixed time horizon τ > 0. Accordingly, we define the initial set of histories ˆH as the set of histories reachable by time τ , i.e., ˆH := Sτt=0 Ht.We let Hθ := {ht ∈ ˆH| sm̸ = θ, ∀hm ⊑ ht} to be a set of non-terminal histories for Attacker θ in the unrestricted game. Let Hθf ⊂ ˆH denote the set of final histories for Attacker θ in the restricted game, defined as the histories that either (i) reach the terminal condition (i.e., the corresponding goal) in the original game, or (ii) those that lie on the truncation frontier t = τ . Based on the above two cases, for each type θ, we partition Hθf into the subset that ends the restricted game at the selected goal θ,i.e., Hθ∗

Note that it is unnecessary to solve the LP (9) to find the solution for the Defender’s strategy ˆπ∗, one can extract the Defender’s strategy from the dual variables for the optimality constraints of the primal LP (8). In the next subsection we lay out an algorithm that utilizes the LP in (8) and iteratively expands the restricted game. 

B. Default strategies. 

Default strategies specify the actions outside the restricted game in order to specify the continuation values Uθht+1 at the leaf nodes, i.e. nodes corresponding to histories ht ∈ H θf ,(see (5)). There are two key considerations for our selection of the default strategies. Firstly, we ensure that Uθht are optimistic estimates of the true values from the Attacker’s perspective, which is necessary for the optimality result pre-sented in Proposition 1. To this end, we select the Attacker’s default strategy to be a best response to the Defender’s default strategy. Secondly, we select the Defender’s strategy so that the aforementioned best response can be computed easily. Defining the default strategies explicitly might take a large amount of memory, but in practice they can be defined using some general rule. We set the Defender’s default strategy, denoted as ˜π ∈ Π, as allocation to the goal closest to the Attacker: 

˜πht (θclose ) = 1 , where θclose = arg min  

> ψ∈Θ

d(st, ψ ). (10) In case of a tie, the Defender selects the goal with the smallest index. The Attacker’s default strategy is found as a deterministic best response to ˜π. Since we selected ˜π to depend only on the Attacker’s current location, the Attacker’s best response can be formulated as a shortest path problem on an MDP. The optimal strategy ˜γθht and the value V θ∗(s), ∀s ∈ V of this shortest path problem can be found via standard value iteration and the Bellman equation [18]. 1 This optimal value provides the continuation cost in the restricted game, Uθht =

V θ∗(st).

Algorithm 1 is conceptually similar to the A ∗ search algo-rithm [19]. The continuation cost Uθht acts as an optimistic 

> 1To ensure that the Attacker reaches the goal in finite time, an arbitrarily small number can be added to the stage cost.

heuristic estimate of the remaining cost for the Attacker. It never overestimates the true equilibrium expected cost because Uθht is computed as the Attacker’s best response to the Defender’s default strategy. Therefore, histories that are not reached with positive probability under the current optimal strategy ˆγ∗ cannot reduce the Attacker’s equilibrium cost and can be safely ignored. The equilibrium Attacker’s strategy is given by ˆγ∗, ob-tained from Algorithm 1, i.e., γ∗ = ˆγ∗. The Defender’s equilibrium strategy is given by ˆπ∗ from Algorithm 1 and the default strategy ˜π:

Proposition 1: The output of Algorithm 1 (π∗, γ ∗) con-stitutes a Nash equilibrium of the original unrestricted game: i.e., it satisfies (4). 

Proof. The linear programs (8) and (9) produce strategies for both players that constitute a Nash equilibrium in the restricted game by construction. Moreover, for any Attacker strategy ˆγ such that Pˆγ (ht) = 0 for all ht ∈ H θf , the cost in the restricted game coincides with the cost in the full game for any Defender policy. Since the Attacker alone controls the evolution of the game history, the probability Pˆγ (ht) is independent of the Defender’s strategy. Consequently, the Defender cannot force the game to reach any history ht ∈ H θf , and thus the Defender’s best response in the restricted game is also a best response in the full game. It therefore remains to show that the Attacker has no incentive to use a strategy that generates histories ht ∈Hθf . This property holds once Algorithm 1 converges. The algorithm terminates when the while loop exits, which occurs when every selected Attacker type reaches its intended goal in the restricted game, i.e., when Pˆγ (ht) = 0 for all ht ∈ H θf .Under this condition, any deviation by the Attacker that drives the game to a history ht ∈ H θf incurs a cost that is at least the continuation cost computed for that history. This continuation cost is evaluated assuming a best response by the Attacker to the Defender’s default strategy. Since the Defender indeed uses the default strategy for such histories under π∗, the Attacker cannot obtain a lower cost in the full game by deviating in this manner. Therefore, the Attacker has no incentive to generate histories in Hθf , and (π∗, γ ∗)

constitutes a Nash equilibrium of the full game. □

Note that Algorithm 1 is guaranteed to terminate in finite time if there exists an equilibrium strategy for Attacker such that all histories generated by it reach the goal in bounded time. Although there may be cases where Attacker’s equilibrium strategy must generate histories which do not reach the goal for an arbitrarily long time, this has not occurred in any tested examples, such as those in Section V, and we conjecture that this does not occur. 

D. Discussion 

In equilibrium, the Attacker may assign zero probability to some types θ, i.e., γ∗

> type

(θ|s0) = 0 , because the corresponding goal may not be worth pursuing. Suppose the equilibrium Attacker selects multiple types θ, ψ ∈ Θ with positive probability, i.e., γ∗

> type

(θ|s0) > 0 and γ∗

> type

(ψ|s0) > 0. Then the equilibrium expected costs for these types must be equal. that occur with positive probability under γθ∗ yield the same equilibrium expected cost. In the next section we propose two metrics to quantify deceptive behavior of the Attacker. IV. D ECEPTION METRICS 

To evaluate deceptive behavior in the incomplete information game, we first establish the benchmark value of the complete information game , when the Defender knows the Attacker’s selected goal θ at the start of the game. This benchmark allows us to define two metrics: a) Risk of Deception (RoD) – the expected penalty incurred by the Attacker when a decep-tive strategy is used against a fully informed Defender; and b) Value of Information (VoI) [20] – the relative advantage the Attacker gains from private information compared to the complete information case. Together, these metrics capture the trade-off between risk and benefit in deceptive play. 

Complete Information Game: Suppose the Defender knows the Attacker’s type θ at the start of the game. In this case, the Defender’s optimal strategy is to allocate to θ every time step, while the Attacker’s best response is to follow the shortest path to its goal. The cost to the Attacker θ is therefore the sum of the initial allocation and the distance to 

θ (length of the shortest path to θ). If the Attacker anticipates that its type will be revealed after selection, it will always choose the type minimizing this cost. 

Remark 2 (Complete Information Game Value): The value of the complete information game for type θ is 

¯U θ∗(s0, r θ ) = d(s0, θ ) + rθ , (13) and the overall value is 

¯U ∗(s0, r) = min 

θ∈Θ

¯U θ∗(s0, r θ ). (14) This benchmark provides a natural reference point for defining both RoD and VoI. 

Risk of Deception (RoD): In complete information game, the Attacker’s deceptive strategy that deviates from a shortest path will be suboptimal. Consequently, when the Attacker is unsure whether the Defender does or does not know its private type, the Attacker faces a dilemma: (i) attempt at deceiving the Defender; or (ii) remain on the shortest path. If the Defender knows θ, then the cost of playing γθ is the expected path length, i.e.:

Definition 2: The risk of deception for Attacker θ is the excess cost of playing γθ∗ instead of staying on a shortest path when the Defender knows θ:RoD θ (s0, r) = ˆdγθ∗

(s0, r θ ) − ¯U θ∗(s0, r).

The expected risk of the overall strategy γ∗ is RoD (s0, r) = X

θ∈Θ

type (θ|s0) RoD θ (s0, r).

If RoD θ (s0, r) = 0 , it implies that Attacker type θ always remains on a shortest path. Likewise, RoD (s0, r) = 0 implies that all types selected with non-zero probability stay on shortest paths. Positive RoD values quantify the expected penalty that the Attacker faces when attempting deception against an informed Defender. 

Value of Information (VoI): VoI [20] quantifies the (normalized) advantage that the Attacker gains from having a private information, relative to the complete information benchmark. 

Definition 3: For Attacker θ, the value of information is VoI θ (s0, r) = ¯U θ∗(s0, r) − Jθ (π∗, γ θ∗; s0, r)¯U θ∗(s0, r) .

The overall value of information is VoI (s0, r) = ¯U ∗(s0, r) − J(π∗, γ ∗; s0, r)¯U ∗(s0, r) .

A positive value of information VoI (s0, r) > 0 indicates that private information will provide a positive benefit to the Attacker. Note that ¯U θ∗(s0, r) = 0 occurs only in the degenerate case where the Attacker starts at goal θ with zero initial allocation, i.e., rθ = 0 , and the game immediately ends. Thus we assume that the denominator is nonzero. Otherwise, we define VoI (s0, r) = 0 .The two measures are complementary: RoD highlights the vulnerability of deceptive strategies when they are revealed, while VoI highlights the potential benefit of deception when the Attacker’s type remains private. <latexit sha1_base64="ke9UgQAuhKEf0yW6/wsArOrz60c="></latexit>

V. N UMERICAL ILLUSTRATION 

We illustrate the equilibrium solution in two settings: a realis-tic road network and a grid world. We highlight how optimal deception emerges and how it depends on environment struc-ture and initial conditions. Across both settings we contrast outcomes under asymmetric and complete information by showing the value of the game, VoI, and RoD. 

A. Road network. 

Figure 2 illustrates scenarios on a road-network graph ex-tracted from real map data and presents VoI := VoI (s0, r)

and RoD := RoD (s0, r). Across all cases we set the initial allocations to zero, i.e., rθ = 0 , ∀θ ∈ Θ.In Fig. 2a, the Attacker mixes uniformly over all goals and, conditional on the chosen goal, follows a shortest path toward it. In Fig. 2b goal g1 is relocated. The equilibrium Attacker never pursues g1 and randomizes uniformly over 

g2 and g3. Consequently, VoI decreases relative to Fig. 2a because the path to g1 no longer aids concealment. Note that RoD = 0 in both Fig. 2a and 2b, which means that the paths taken by the equilibrium Attacker are the shortest ones, yet VoI > 0. This highlights the benefit the equilibrium Attacker receives from the Defender’s informational disadvantage. Figures 2c and 2d add a new goal g4. On Fig. 2c the Attacker selects only g4 and follows the shortest path. Here VoI = 0 , yet the Attacker’s total cost is lower than in Fig. 2a, illustrating that VoI does not directly track the equilibrium payoff. In Fig. 2d, the placement of g4 induces uniform mixing over all four goals, with routes to g1 and g4 transiting through g3. Here the Attacker chooses a non-shortest path to g1, yielding RoD > 0, while VoI has the highest value among the four cases. 

B. Grid world. 

Figure 3 presents a grid-world scenario with three goals. All values depict equilibrium performance under rθ = 0 , ∀θ ∈

Θ. The bottom-right subfigure shows that when the Attacker starts close to a particular goal, it often commits to that goal <latexit sha1_base64="64N3gODN+f/0ufCpv18eQ0L0Vzk="></latexit>

alone. The game then becomes equivalent to a complete-information case, which is reflected by VoI being zero, as shown in the top-right subfigure. In contrast, the dark-green region (labeled as {g1, g 2, g 3}) away from all three goals lead to mixing between all three goals. Concealing the intent provides a tangible advantage captured by positive VoI ,and the Attacker often accepts non-shortest path detours, as evidenced by positive RoD at certain initial positions. VI. C ONCLUSION 

We formulated a new variant of the deceptive path plan-ning (DPP) game with goal selection, and introduced a solution method that integrates linear programming with the Double-Oracle algorithm. We propose a particular choice of default strategies that allow us to solve DPP game which has indefinite horizon, and it also ensures our solution is an exact Nash equilibrium, rather than an ε-Nash. We present two complementary metrics to quantify the benefits and costs of deception: Value of Information (VoI) and Risk of Deception (RoD). Together, these measures delineate conditions where deception is both effective and risk-averse. The illustrative examples reveal how deceptive behavior can improve performance. Future work will address scenarios with partial observability, stochastic dynamics and games in continuous space. REFERENCES [1] M. Lloyd, The Art of Military Deception . Pen and Sword, 2003. [2] M.
